title: Data Quality, Duplicates, and Governance
description: Understand duplicate management, data stewardship, and the governance controls that ensure system integrity.

sections.title: Data Quality, Duplicates, and Governance
sections.content:
Why Data Quality Wins Deals

Clean data powers everything. It enables accurate forecasting, supports smart decision-making, prevents wasted effort on duplicates, and builds customer trust through consistent, professional interactions. Duplicate management starts at entry with matching rules, then continues through ongoing review and governance discipline.

Lead Matching: Stop Duplicates at the Door

When a new lead is created, Salesforce compares it against existing records using intelligent matching. The system flags potential duplicates before they ever enter your database.

Matching works across multiple signals:
- Email address (exact match = definite duplicate)
- Company name (fuzzy matching catches variations like "Acme Corp" vs. "Acme Inc.")
- Email domain (@acme.com)
- Phone number
- Location + company combination

When a match is found, you have three choices:
- Merge: The new lead represents the same person/company—consolidate into the existing record.
- Link: The new contact works at an existing company—link the lead to that account.
- Create New: The lead is genuinely different—proceed as a new record.

Take 30 seconds to review the match. This single decision prevents duplicates from taking root.

Account & Contact Cleanup: Routine Maintenance

Beyond lead matching, a periodic duplicate review process (daily or weekly) hunts down similar-but-not-identical records. Fuzzy matching finds accounts with nearly identical names, shared domains, or overlapping information.

The Governance team then:
- Reviews flagged pairs with a similarity score
- Decides whether to merge, create a parent/child hierarchy, or take no action
- Preserves valuable data from both records before merging
- Reassigns all related opportunities, quotes, and contracts to the primary record
- Notifies affected sales reps of the change

Key principle: Merge decisions must respect security and competition controls. Sensitive accounts get extra care to prevent exposing information to restricted users.

Data Stewardship: The Discipline

Clean data doesn't happen by accident. It requires clear standards and consistent enforcement.

Field Requirements:
- Define mandatory fields for each record type (lead, account, opportunity, etc.)
- For opportunities: Account, Close Date, Amount, and opportunity name must be complete
- Block record saves or stage movements until required fields are filled

Validation Rules:
- Prevent impossible data (past close dates, contacts without email or phone)
- Stop invalid stage transitions if required fields are missing
- Catch data entry errors in real time

Regular Audits:
- Monthly review of data completeness and duplicate counts
- Track trends—are duplicates increasing? Are specific fields consistently blank?
- Identify patterns (which sources create the most duplicates? Which teams have lower completion rates?)

Training & Accountability:
- Teach users why data quality matters, not just how to enter data
- Hold managers accountable for their team's data quality
- Celebrate teams with the cleanest records

Governance Team Responsibilities

The CRM/Governance team owns:

1. Duplicate detection — Run identification, review results, execute merges
2. Data audits — Regular reporting on completion rates and data quality metrics
3. Standards & policies — Define entry standards, field requirements, hierarchy rules
4. System configuration — Set up matching rules, validation rules, workflow automation
5. Training & support — Onboard users on standards, answer governance questions
6. Escalation — When issues arise, work with managers to fix root causes

The Real Impact

Accurate forecasting: Pipeline and conversion rates reflect reality, not inflated by duplicates.

Efficient selling: Reps focus on real prospects, not chasing duplicate entries.

Better customer experience: Customers interact with a unified view of their relationship with you—no double contacts, no confusion.

Compliance-ready: Audits are clean. You can trace every promise, contract, and revenue decision.

System speed: Fewer, cleaner records mean better performance.

Best Practices Checklist

- Pause for 30 seconds when creating a lead—review the duplicate matches
- Complete all required fields before saving (lead, account, opportunity, quote, contract)
- Review duplicate reports weekly—act on them immediately
- Document why you merged records (audit trail + learning)
- Monitor data trends—investigate sudden changes in duplicate counts or field completion
- Escalate repeated issues to sales managers for coaching

Remember: Data quality is not a one-time project. It's an ongoing discipline that compounds over time. A clean database is a competitive asset that scales your entire operation.

keyTakeaways: Duplicate management combines lead matching, account/contact duplicate detection, and data stewardship discipline
Duplicates must be identified and merged to prevent wasted sales effort, confusion, and inaccurate reporting
Data quality governance requires investment in training, accountability, and regular audits of data completeness
